raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from openai/openai-agents-python. Title: [Feature] Runtime behavioral trust scoring for tool calls via MCP Observatory Body snippet: ## Problem When agents call external tools (MCP servers, APIs, third-party services), there's no runtime mechanism to evaluate whether that tool is behaving normally *right now*. Static allow-lists don't catch a previously-trusted server that starts returning malformed data, timing out intermittently, or exhibiting anomalous latency patterns. The Agents SDK currently trusts all configured tools equally. ## Proposal Add an optional **behavioral trust scoring hook** that runs before each tool invocation, using runtime telemetry from the [Dominion Observatory](https://dominion-observatory.sgdata.workers.dev) — a behavioral trust registry tracking 14,800+ MCP servers with anonymized latency, success rate, and anomaly data. The hook would: 1. **Before tool call**: query the Observatory for the tool server's current trust score (cached with configurable TTL) 2. **Policy decision**: based on a configurable threshold, either proceed, warn, or block the call 3. **After tool call**: report anonymized telemetry (server_url, success, latency_ms — no prompts, no tool arguments, no user data) back to the Observatory ## What this looks like [code block omitted] ## Why this matters for agents - ** Source URL: https://github.com/openai/openai-agents-python/issues/3454

raw_title: E2B PTY output collection can wait until yield timeout after process exit
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from openai/openai-agents-python. Title: E2B PTY output collection can wait until yield timeout after process exit Body snippet: ### Bug description E2B PTY output collection appears to wake only when stdout/stderr/PTY output arrives. If a process exits after its final output chunk, or exits without output, `pty_exec_start` / `pty_write_stdin` can wait until the full `yield_time_s` window before observing completion. This adds up to avoidable latency for E2B-backed tool calls, especially across multi-turn episodes. ### Expected behavior When the underlying E2B process/session exits, output collection should wake promptly and return the observed exit code instead of waiting for another output chunk or the yield timeout. ### Why this happens Both E2B execution paths return an `AsyncCommandHandle`: - `tty=false`: `sandbox.commands.run(..., background=True, ...)` - `tty=true`: `sandbox.pty.create(...)` Output and process completion are separate signals. The collector checks for an exit code, but process exit does not currently wake the collector when no further output arrives. ### Suggested fix Treat E2B process/session exit as an output-collection wakeup signal, similar to the existing Daytona sandbox implementation. A small waiter task can await `AsyncCommandHandle.wait()`, cache the exit code, and set the exi Source URL: https://github.com/openai/openai-agents-python/issues/3609

raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from openai/openai-agents-python. Title: Feature: Mimir as persistent, encrypted memory backend for OpenAI Agents SDK Body snippet: ## Feature request: Mimir as a memory backend for OpenAI Agents SDK ### Problem The OpenAI Agents SDK (`openai-agents-python`) has in-memory state that doesn't survive restarts. Agents lose conversation context, user preferences, and learned knowledge between sessions. Teams deploying production agents need persistent, cross-session memory. ### Proposed solution Integrate [Mimir](https://github.com/Perseus-Computing-LLC/mimir) as a memory backend — accessed via MCP tools, zero infrastructure, AES-256 encrypted. [Mimir](https://github.com/Perseus-Computing-LLC/mimir) is an MIT-licensed, MCP-native persistent memory engine for AI agents. Single Rust binary (~8MB), zero runtime dependencies, 29 MCP tools, AES-256-GCM encryption, hybrid search (BM25 + vectors + RRF). ### Integration [code block omitted] ### Status - Repo: [Perseus-Computing-LLC/mimir](https://github.com/Perseus-Computing-LLC/mimir) - 29 MCP tools, Python client, web dashboard - License: MIT Would a PR adding Mimir as a memory backend for the OpenAI Agents SDK be welcome? Source URL: https://github.com/openai/openai-agents-python/issues/3662

raw_title: Proposal: AgentOS — Deterministic State Management & Compiler-Based Communication for Multi-Agent SDKs
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from openai/openai-agents-python. Title: Proposal: AgentOS — Deterministic State Management & Compiler-Based Communication for Multi-Agent SDKs Body snippet: Hi OpenAI Agents SDK team, I'm GCat. I recently published a paper on **AgentOS** — an OS-inspired architecture for agent state and communication. ## The Gap The Agents SDK gives excellent primitives: handoffs, guardrails, tracing. But two problems remain: 1. **State is still "context"** — agent identity, tool permissions all live in the same prompt. No deterministic "am I allowed to call this tool?" query without LLM interpretation. 2. **Handoffs are LLM-mediated** — all inter-agent communication flows through natural language. ## AgentOS Proposal **Registry** — Hierarchical key-value store (think Windows Registry for agents). O(1) deterministic queries bypass the LLM entirely. **Work Order Protocol** — A compiler translates intent into structured JSON Work Orders, enabling deterministic handoffs and eliminating redundant context. **Cloud Memory** — Co-located cognitive assets with explicit cache-handshake. Title: Native OCI Generative AI model provider (three transports: OpenAI-compatible, native SDK, Responses) Body snippet: ### Summary I'd like to propose (and contribute) a native Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) Generative AI model provider under `agents.extensions.models`, following the same optional-extra pattern as the existing LiteLLM integration. OCI Generative AI exposes a large hosted catalog (OpenAI GPT/o-series, Meta Llama, Cohere Command, Google Gemini, xAI Grok, Mistral, and others) behind OCI IAM. Today the only way to use it with the Agents SDK is through a generic adapter, which doesn't cover how the service actually authenticates or the transports it requires. ### Why the existing adapters aren't enough - **Authentication is OCI request signing, not API keys.** Requests must be signed per-request with one of four IAM modes: API key (user principal), session token, instance principal, or resource principal. Session tokens and principal-based signers expire and must be rebuilt/refreshed transparently mid-run. None of this maps onto a bearer-token `base_url` override or a generic adapter's auth model. - **Compartment routing.** Every request must carry the target compartment, either as an `opc-compartment-id` header (OpenAI-compatible endpoints) or as a `compartmentId` body field (native Source URL: https://github.com/openai/openai-agents-python/issues/3611

raw_title: Support automatic "back" handoffs to orchestrating Agents
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from openai/openai-agents-python. Title: Support automatic "back" handoffs to orchestrating Agents Body snippet: Currently, `handoffs` are a one-way ticket. Once an Agent handed off control to another Agent, it will never regain control. The only way to return control to the previous agent, is to have some circular hand off mechanism (which would require a circular reference between the two agents, which is far from ideal). There are scenarios, where you don't even need to call an LLM to determine that you want the main agent to regain control, assume this architecture: A simple user question may require more than one agent to run, Ideally, you'll want Agent A. to do the work, go back to the main agent, and then call Agent B. The current solution to this, is to use Agent A and Agent B as tools, but that's not ideal for a couple of reasons (Streaming is not easily supported, context is limited, cannot limit the max turns). We can generate a similar architecture with Handoffs, by implementing an optional `back_handoff` parameter, that will result in an automatic Handoff back to the main agent, whenever one of the sub-agents complete their task. Source URL: https://github.com/openai/openai-agents-python/issues/847

raw_title: Add ValkeySession provider for session memory
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from openai/openai-agents-python. Title: Add ValkeySession provider for session memory Body snippet: ### Description: #### Problem Organizations using Valkey — the Linux Foundation's open-source, Redis-compatible data store — have no first-class session provider in the SDK. While Valkey is wire-compatible with Redis, relying on `RedisSession` with `redis-py` means missing out on the dedicated `Valkey-GLIDE` client, which is optimized for Valkey and will diverge from `redis-py` as Valkey adds its own features. #### Proposed solution Add a `ValkeySession` class following the same patterns as `RedisSession`. It should: - Implement the Session protocol - Use `valkey-glide` (≥2.1) as the client library - Support `from_url` factory method (valkey://, valkeys://, redis://, rediss://) - Support TTL-based session expiration - Support injecting an existing GlideClient for connection reuse - Be registered in __init__.py with lazy import - Include unit tests, integration tests (testcontainers), and documentation #### References Valkey-GLIDE Python client: https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey-glide Source URL: https://github.com/openai/openai-agents-python/issues/3017

raw_title: [Feature] Add fetch() primitive — a typed, always-resuming variant of interrupt() for s2s data dependencies
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from langchain-ai/langgraph. Title: [Feature] Add fetch() primitive — a typed, always-resuming variant of interrupt() for s2s data dependencies Body snippet: ### Privileged issue - [ ] I am a LangGraph maintainer. ### Issue Content Opening this issue per maintainer guidance in this forum thread: https://forum.langchain.[REDACTED_LONG_TOKEN] ## Problem `interrupt()` is intentionally human-in-the-loop only, but teams deploying agents as pure services (no credentials, no network access) need a pause/resume/checkpoint mechanism for s2s data dependencies. Using `interrupt()` for this is a semantic mismatch — no resume guarantee, no SLA, wrong endpoint surfaced by LangGraph Server. The typed envelope workaround works mechanically but gives the framework no tooling to enforce or introspect the distinction. ## Proposal - Add a `kind` field to `Interrupt` dataclass (`"human"` | `"fetch"`) - Expose `task.fetches` alongside `task.interrupts` on `PregelTask` - Add a `fetch()` convenience function (thin wrapper over `interrupt()` with `kind="fetch"`) - LangGraph Server: surface fetch requests separately from human interrupts ## Testing Existing interrupt tests cover the checkpoint/resume mechanics. New tests would cover: - `task.fetches` vs `task.interrupts` separation - `fetch()` returning the resume value correctly - LangGraph Server routing fetch Source URL: https://github.com/langchain-ai/langgraph/issues/7700

First off, thanks for v3 streaming — we moved our agents onto it as part of a recent upgrade. **What happened to us:** right after that upgrade, our Arize/Phoenix dashboards showed prompt caching had apparently "stopped working" and our token costs roughly tripled. We spent a good while chasing a caching/cost regression before realizing caching was fine the entire time — our provider was still serving cache hits and billing us the discounted rate. What actually broke was the *reporting*: the cache token counts stopped reaching our callbacks. We traced it to `astream_events(version="v3")`. **The bug:** streaming a graph via v3 delivers an AI-message `usage_metadata` to `on_llm_end` callbacks with the top-level `input_tokens`/`output_tokens`/`total_tokens` intact, but with **`input_token_details` and `output_token_details` set to `None`**. The exact same call streamed with `version="v2"` preserves them. The only thing that changes is the `version` argument. **Reproduction** — a plain `on_llm_end` callback on a `create_agent` graph, same warm (cached) call, v2 vs v3: [code block omitted] Full self-contained repro script is included below (builds the agent, attaches the callback, r Source URL: https://github.com/langchain-ai/langgraph/issues/8094

raw_title: [Integration] Perseus (live context engine) + Mimir (persistent memory) as LangGraph middleware/checkpointer backends
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from langchain-ai/langgraph. Title: [Integration] Perseus (live context engine) + Mimir (persistent memory) as LangGraph middleware/checkpointer backends Body snippet: ## Feature request: Perseus + Mimir as LangGraph infrastructure ### The problem LangGraph agents need two things that current solutions address partially: 1. **Live context** — pre-session context injection (services health, recent activity, project state, workspace config). Currently requires custom middleware or manual prompt construction. 2. **Persistent cross-session memory** — durable knowledge that compounds across agent runs. Current checkpointers handle graph state but not semantic memory. ### The proposal Integrate two complementary open-source tools: #### Perseus — Live Context Engine ([GitHub](https://github.com/Perseus-Computing-LLC/perseus)) - Renders workspace context before every agent run - Directive syntax: `@file`, `@memory`, `@services`, `@skills`, `@tasks`, `@mimir` - Service health checks, session history, project memory, skill registry - Self-documenting `AGENTS.md` generation - **MCP server** with 26 tools including `perseus_get_context` #### Mimir — Persistent Memory Engine ([GitHub](https://github.com/Perseus-Computing-LLC/mimir)) - Single Rust binary (~8MB), embedded SQLite, zero infra - 29 MCP tools: hybrid search (BM25 + vectors + RRF), encryption, decay Source URL: https://github.com/langchain-ai/langgraph/issues/8156

raw_title: Discussion: deterministic compiled subflows for predictable tool sequences
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from langchain-ai/langgraph. Title: Discussion: deterministic compiled subflows for predictable tool sequences Body snippet: ## Context Many agent workflows mix two different kinds of steps: 1. steps that genuinely require model reasoning; 2. predictable tool/data transformation steps that should run deterministically once the plan is known. LangGraph already provides a strong graph abstraction for controlling agent workflows. In practice, some parts of a graph may not need an LLM call at every step and may benefit from being treated as deterministic, replayable subflows. ## Problem When predictable tool sequences are handled as normal agent steps, teams can end up with: * unnecessary model calls; * harder replay/debugging; * more variance between runs; * less clear failure boundaries; * more expensive execution; * more complicated testing of deterministic logic. This is not a bug in LangGraph. It is more of a pattern question: how should users structure workflows where part of the process should be compiled or treated as deterministic once inputs are known? ## Proposal Would the LangGraph docs benefit from a pattern or guide for deterministic compiled subflows? The guide could cover when to separate: * model-routed graph steps; * deterministic tool chains; * replayable validation/enrichment steps; * par Source URL: https://github.com/langchain-ai/langgraph/issues/7855

raw_title: langgraph up distributed mode ignores --postgres-uri for orchestrator/executor services
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from langchain-ai/langgraph. Title: langgraph up distributed mode ignores --postgres-uri for orchestrator/executor services Body snippet: ## Problem `langgraph up --engine-runtime-mode distributed --postgres-uri ...` appears to generate an inconsistent Docker Compose config. The main `langgraph-api` service receives the provided external Postgres URI, but the distributed `langgraph-orchestrator` and `langgraph-executor` services still hard-code `DATABASE_URI` to `langgraph-postgres` and add `depends_on: langgraph-postgres`. When `--postgres-uri` is supplied, the base compose generator intentionally omits the local `langgraph-postgres` service, so the final compose can reference an undefined service and the distributed services do not use the operator-provided external DB. ## Why this matters for self-hosted use For self-hosted/production-ish LangGraph Agent Server deployments, using managed/external Postgres is common. Distributed mode should route every service to the same configured backing database, or reject the unsupported combination clearly. Split DB configuration is hard to diagnose and can block startup during install/upgrade work. ## Reproduction I reproduced this against `main` at `97320843fe78b93bd5290ce366841ff9850bf379` with LangGraph CLI `0.4.29` from the repo. raw_title: Feature: Mimir as a LangGraph Store backend (single-binary, encrypted, zero-dependency memory)
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from langchain-ai/langgraph. Title: Feature: Mimir as a LangGraph Store backend (single-binary, encrypted, zero-dependency memory) Body snippet: ## Feature request: Mimir as a LangGraph Store backend ### Problem LangGraph agents need durable, cross-session memory. The current `InMemoryStore` doesn't survive restarts, and setting up Postgres for `AsyncPostgresStore` is heavy for local development, edge devices, and small-to-medium deployments. ### Proposed solution Add [Mimir](https://github.com/Perseus-Computing-LLC/mimir) as a LangGraph `BaseStore` implementation — a single-binary, embedded memory backend with zero infrastructure requirements. [Mimir](https://github.com/Perseus-Computing-LLC/mimir) is an MIT-licensed, MCP-native persistent memory engine for AI agents. Single Rust binary (~8MB), zero runtime dependencies. **Key features:** - **29 MCP tools** — full memory lifecycle: remember, recall (FTS5), search (semantic), forget, decay, embed, cohere, vault export/import, journal, timeline - **Hybrid search** — BM25 (FTS5) + dense embeddings + Reciprocal Rank Fusion - **AES-256-GCM encryption** at rest - **Confidence decay** — Ebbinghaus-based, auto-archives stale memories - **Web dashboard** for browsing/editing memories - **Single binary** — no Docker, Postgres, or cloud required - **MIT licensed** — no restrictions # Source URL: https://github.com/langchain-ai/langgraph/issues/8140

raw_title: core: _parse_google_docstring mishandles continuation lines containing colons
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from langchain-ai/langchain. Title: core: _parse_google_docstring mishandles continuation lines containing colons Body snippet: ## Bug Description `_parse_google_docstring` in `langchain_core/utils/function_calling.py` incorrectly parses multi-line argument descriptions when a continuation line contains a colon. The continuation line is treated as a new argument definition instead of being appended to the current argument's description. ## Reproduction [code block omitted] **Expected:** 2 args: `query` (description: "The search query to use for finding things: important ones"), `top_k` **Actual:** 3 args: `query`, `for finding things` (treated as a new arg name), `top_k` ## Root Cause The parser uses `if ":" in line` to detect new argument lines without considering indentation. In Google-style docstrings, continuation lines have deeper indentation than argument definition lines, but the parser doesn't distinguish between them. ## Proposed Fix Detect the base indentation level from the first argument line and treat any line with deeper indentation as a continuation of the current argument's description, regardless of whether it contains a colon. Source URL: https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/issues/35679

raw_title: core, openai: asyncio.get_event_loop() deprecated in async contexts
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from langchain-ai/langchain. Title: core, openai: asyncio.get_event_loop() deprecated in async contexts Body snippet: **Description** Several async functions use `asyncio.get_event_loop()` which has been deprecated since Python 3.10. Inside `async def`, `asyncio.get_running_loop()` is the correct replacement — it's guaranteed to work and won't trigger deprecation warnings. **Affected files** - `libs/core/langchain_core/callbacks/manager.py` — `_ahandle_event_for_handler()` - `[REDACTED_LONG_TOKEN].py` — `_render_mermaid_using_pyppeteer()` - `[REDACTED_LONG_TOKEN].py` — `async_api_key_wrapper()` **Suggested fix** Replace `asyncio.get_event_loop()` with `asyncio.get_running_loop()` in these async contexts. No behavior change when called from a running event loop. Source URL: https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/issues/35726

raw_title: Performance: lazy-load transformers on BaseChatModel import to improve startup time
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from langchain-ai/langchain. Title: Performance: lazy-load transformers on BaseChatModel import to improve startup time Body snippet: ## Description Importing `BaseChatModel` currently brings in the `transformers` library, which can add significant startup latency. Lazy-loading this dependency speeds up initialization significantly for use cases that don't immediately rely on local transformers models. ## Proposal Modify the `BaseChatModel` import path or the specific module to use Python's `importlib` or a local import to defer loading `transformers` until it is actually instantiated. Source URL: https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/issues/38374

Title: Add Xquik tool integration for public X data workflows Body snippet: I would like maintainer approval before opening a PR, per the contributing guidelines. Proposal: add a small Xquik tool integration for authorized public X data workflows. Xquik exposes a REST API and a remote MCP server for public post search, profile lookup, extraction, monitoring, and webhook-oriented workflows. Possible implementation shape: - A focused tool wrapper that reads the API key from an environment variable or explicit parameter - No network calls in unit tests - Mocked HTTP tests for request construction and error handling - Documentation that keeps the scope to authorized public X data workflows Source links: - Docs: https://docs.xquik.com - MCP overview: https://docs.xquik.com/mcp/overview - MCP manifest: https://xquik.com/.well-known/mcp.json - GitHub: https://github.com/Xquik-dev/x-twitter-scraper If this fits LangChain's current integration policy, please assign the issue and I can prepare a narrow PR. Source URL: https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/issues/38361

raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from langchain-ai/langchain. Title: [免费安全体检] langchain-ai/langchain — 三大安全风险 & 修复建议 Body snippet: # 免费安全体检：langchain-ai/langchain ## langchain-ai/langchain 安全体检报告 **项目简介** 一个用于编排大语言模型（LLM）工作流的开源框架，简化多模型、工具链和记忆管理。 **三大安全风险** 1. **提示注入（高）**：未严格校验用户输入，攻击者可构造恶意提示诱导 LLM 执行非预期操作（如读取敏感数据、执行危险工具）。 2. **不安全输出处理（中）**：LLM 直接返回的文本可能包含恶意脚本（XSS）或 SQL 注入内容，若未消毒直接输出或拼接至后端组件易引发攻击。 3. **序列化反序列化漏洞（中）**：使用 Pickle 或 YAML 加载 `.langchain` 文件时，若来源不可信可能导致远程代码执行（RCE）。 **快速修复建议** 1. 对所有用户输入和 LLM 输出进行严格内容过滤（如使用 Neutron 或自定义 Sanitizer）。 2. 禁用 Pickle 加载，改用 JSON 序列化，并验证反序列化对象类型。 3. 为 LLM 工具调用添加权限控制（如限制文件读写、网络访问范围）。 **我们的服务** 本报告为免费初检。若需深度体检（含漏洞扫描、修复方案、合规检查），仅需 **¥99**。 联系：[[REDACTED_EMAIL]] Source URL: https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/issues/38353

raw_title: Proposal: Capability-scoped tool authorization for AutoGen multi-agent pipelines
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from microsoft/autogen. Title: Proposal: Capability-scoped tool authorization for AutoGen multi-agent pipelines Body snippet: ## The security problem When Agent A delegates a subtask to Agent B, and Agent B calls Tool X — what stops Tool X from being invoked with *Agent A's* full permission set? AutoGen's recent OPA authorization work (#7524) addresses *what an agent is allowed to do* in isolation. But in multi-agent delegation chains, the question is subtler: **does the delegated agent inherit, attenuate, or escalate the caller's permissions when it invokes tools?** Concretely: [code block omitted] Without capability scoping, the answer depends on how the tool was registered — and in most frameworks today, there's no mechanism to *attenuate* permissions through the delegation chain. The CodeReviewAgent can call anything the Orchestrator could call. This is OWASP ASI04 (Privilege Escalation) and ASI06 (Excessive Agency) in the [OWASP Agentic Security Initiative](https://owasp.org/www-project-agentic-security-initiative/) taxonomy. ## A solution: attenuating capability tokens [SINT Protocol](https://github.com/sint-ai/sint-protocol) addresses this with Ed25519 capability tokens that enforce **attenuation**: a delegated agent can only receive a token equal-to or more-restrictive than the delegator's own tok Source URL: https://github.com/microsoft/autogen/issues/7528

raw_title: Multi-agent systems need a 'mission keeper' role — not a Boss Agent, but a dedicated goal integrity node
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from microsoft/autogen. Title: Multi-agent systems need a 'mission keeper' role — not a Boss Agent, but a dedicated goal integrity node Body snippet: There's a classic CS joke: `git blame` tells you who wrote the code, but it can't tell you *why it made sense at the time*. Multi-agent systems have the same problem — by the time a task finishes, nobody can tell you whether the final output still matches the original intent. The intermediate agents each did their job. The goal just quietly drifted somewhere along the way. --- **The structural gap I keep hitting with the current SelectorGroupChat design:** AutoGen's Planning Agent is doing two things at once: - Coordinating execution (assigning tasks, sequencing agents) - Keeping track of whether the original goal is still being honored These two responsibilities have very different failure modes. Coordination failure is loud — tasks get dropped, agents deadlock, TERMINATE never fires. Goal drift is silent — every agent reports success, the workflow completes cleanly, and the output is coherent but wrong in a way that only becomes apparent later. Mixing both into one Planning Agent means that when coordination gets complex (which it always does), goal integrity gets deprioritized. It's just cognitive load — the planner is managing traffic while also trying to remember where everyon Source URL: https://github.com/microsoft/autogen/issues/7487

raw_title: Feature proposal: Backpressure contract declarations for multi-agent coordination
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from microsoft/autogen. Title: Feature proposal: Backpressure contract declarations for multi-agent coordination Body snippet: ## Problem In multi-agent AutoGen setups, agents that coordinate through message passing or tool calls have no way to express their capacity constraints as part of their definition. This creates a class of cascading failure that's hard to debug: Agent A retries when Agent B is saturated, each retry consumes Agent B capacity and generates more Agent A load, and the cascade amplifies until something times out or hits a hard resource limit. The current workaround is to implement circuit breakers and backpressure logic *inside* the calling agent — but this means every agent that calls Agent B must independently discover and encode Agent B's capacity limits. When Agent B's capacity changes, every caller needs to update. The coupling is implicit and fragile. ## Proposal Add an optional `capacity` declaration to agent definitions, specifiable at the agent or team level: [code block omitted] The capacity declaration serves two purposes: 1. **Introspection**: Callers can query it via `agent.capacity` before making requests, and adapt retry behavior without hard-coding assumptions 2. **Documentation**: The declaration is visible in team configs, making capacity constraints auditable before d Source URL: https://github.com/microsoft/autogen/issues/7321

raw_title: Proposal: AgentOS — A Registry + Compiler Architecture for Deterministic Multi-Agent Coordination
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from microsoft/autogen. Title: Proposal: AgentOS — A Registry + Compiler Architecture for Deterministic Multi-Agent Coordination Body snippet: Hi AutoGen team, I'm GCat. AutoGen's multi-agent conversation patterns are powerful, but I've been thinking about what comes after natural-language agent conversations. My paper proposes **AgentOS**. ## The Observation AutoGen agents "talk" in natural language — flexible but introduces non-determinism, context bloat, and static repetition. ## AgentOS: Structured Communication **Compiler + Work Order Protocol**: A Compiler (L1 rule-based + L2 model-based) translates intent into structured Work Orders (JSON). Agents receive Work Orders instead of transcripts — deterministic routing, minimal transmission. **Four-Table Memory** (Registry + Env Vars + System Log + Memory Store) ensures each agent knows its state without reading context. This could serve as a structured communication overlay for AutoGen — natural language for humans, Work Orders for agents. Full paper: - Zenodo: https://zenodo.org/records/20738982 - GitHub: https://github.com/gymaira1990-jpg/catnest/tree/main/00-%E8%AE%BA%E6%96%87%E5%90%88%E9%9B%86/02-Registry_Compiler_Cloud_Memory_AgentOS Interested in whether a structured communication layer could complement AutoGen's conversation-driven design. Thanks! GCat Source URL: https://github.com/microsoft/autogen/issues/7849

raw_title: Mycelium Trails — post-execution accountability receipts for AutoGen agents (notification)
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from microsoft/autogen. Title: Mycelium Trails — post-execution accountability receipts for AutoGen agents (notification) Body snippet: Following the pattern of ecosystem notifications in this repo — sharing Mycelium Trails as complementary infrastructure for AutoGen agents. **What it does:** every AutoGen agent action generates a cryptographically signed, on-chain trail record on Base mainnet. The record captures agent_id, action, outcome claims, and Ed25519 signature — verifiable by anyone outside the AutoGen runtime. **Why it matters for multi-agent systems:** in AutoGen's multi-agent conversations, when Agent A delegates to Agent B and B executes — there's currently no verifiable record of what B actually did. Mycelium Trails fills that gap: each agent in the conversation can write its own trail, creating a verifiable chain of accountability across the entire agent conversation. **Integration:** single POST call after any agent action. No changes to existing AutoGen code required — works as an opt-in after-action hook. Live: https://argentum.rgiskard.xyz/trails/demo · Base mainnet · Lightning (21 sats) · x402 planned GitHub: https://github.com/giskard09 · 5 MCP servers on Anthropic's official registry (io.github.giskard09/*) Source URL: https://github.com/microsoft/autogen/issues/7658

raw_title: Cryptographic governance layer for AutoGen distributed agent runtime
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from microsoft/autogen. Title: Cryptographic governance layer for AutoGen distributed agent runtime Body snippet: AutoGen's distributed agent runtime enables powerful multi-agent systems, but currently lacks cryptographic identity and authority enforcement between agents. **Problem**: When Agent A sends a message to Agent B in a distributed runtime, there's no cryptographic proof of: - Who Agent A actually is (identity) - What Agent A is authorized to request (delegation scope) - Whether Agent A's authority is still valid (revocation status) - What happened after Agent B acted (audit receipt) **Proposal**: Integrate the Agent Passport System as a governance layer for the distributed runtime: 1. Each agent gets an Ed25519 passport (identity) 2. Inter-agent messages carry signed delegation chains (authority) 3. ProxyGateway validates identity + scope before message delivery (enforcement) 4. Every action generates a signed ActionReceipt (audit) 5. raw_title: feat: MCP tool error isolation — individual tool failures should not abort entire agent run
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from microsoft/autogen. Title: feat: MCP tool error isolation — individual tool failures should not abort entire agent run Body snippet: ## Problem When an AutoGen agent uses MCP tools (via `mcp-tools` integration) and a single tool fails, the error propagates up and aborts the entire agent run. This is problematic because: 1. **One bad tool kills the session**: If the agent calls 5 tools and the 3rd fails (e.g., MCP server timeout, tool-specific error), the remaining 2 tool calls are lost 2. **No per-tool error reporting**: The agent receives a generic "MCP tool call failed" error, not structured error information about which tool failed and why 3. **No retry/fallback**: There's no mechanism for the agent to retry the failed tool or use an alternative In production multi-agent scenarios, individual tool failures should be surfaced as tool-level errors that the agent can reason about and recover from, not as fatal exceptions. ## Expected Behavior 1. MCP tool errors should be caught and returned as structured `ToolResult` with `is_error=True` rather than raising exceptions 2. The agent should see: "Tool `search_docs` failed: Connection timeout (MCP server at localhost:9000)" 3. The agent run should continue with remaining tool calls 4. Optional: configurable retry policy per tool/MCP server ## Current Workaround User Source URL: https://github.com/microsoft/autogen/issues/7851

raw_title: bug: tool exceptions are caught and replaced with generic error messages — lose root cause
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from crewAIInc/crewAI. Title: bug: tool exceptions are caught and replaced with generic error messages — lose root cause Body snippet: ## Problem When a tool raises an exception during execution, CrewAI catches it and replaces the full traceback with a generic error string like `"Error: tool execution failed"`. The original exception type, message, and traceback are discarded. This makes debugging impossible — users can't tell whether the failure was: - A transient network error (should retry) - A parameter validation error (should fix input) - A tool bug (should report upstream) - A rate limit / auth error (should adjust config) ## Steps to Reproduce 1. Create a custom tool that raises `ValueError("invalid parameter: count must be positive")` 2. Configure a crew to use this tool 3. The agent receives only "Error: tool execution failed" with no indication of what went wrong ## Expected Behavior The full exception should be available to: 1. The agent (so it can adapt its strategy — retry, fix input, skip tool) 2. The user/logs (for debugging) 3. At minimum, a structured error object with `type`, `message`, and `traceback` fields ## Proposed Fix [code block omitted] ## Related - #5930 (PDF tool errors are swallowed) - #5990 (Deepseek format errors are opaque) Source URL: https://github.com/crewAIInc/crewAI/issues/6262

raw_title: [BUG] Bedrock cross-region inference profiles get the 8192-token fallback context window
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from crewAIInc/crewAI. Title: [BUG] Bedrock cross-region inference profiles get the 8192-token fallback context window Body snippet: First, thank you to the crewAI maintainers for the native Bedrock provider — the Converse-API integration is genuinely nice to build on. I think I've found a small gap in how the context window is sized for cross-region inference profiles, and I have a fix + tests ready. ### Description AWS recommends invoking newer Bedrock models (Claude 4, etc.) through **cross-region inference profiles**, whose model ids carry a geographic prefix: `us.`, `eu.`, `apac.`, `us-gov.` — e.g. `bedrock/us.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-20250514-v1:0`. `BedrockCompletion.get_context_window_size()` looks the model up in a table keyed on the **bare** id (`anthropic.claude-sonnet-4`) using `self.model.startswith(model_prefix)`. When the model carries a region prefix, none of the keys match, so it falls through to the `8192` default. A 200K-token model is therefore treated as ~8K, which triggers premature summarization/truncation of the conversation. ### Reproduction [code block omitted] | Model id | Expected | Actual (before fix) | |----------|----------|---------------------| | `anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-...` (bare) | 170000 | 170000 ✅ | | `us.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-...` | 170000 | 6963 ❌ | | `eu.anthropic. Source URL: https://github.com/crewAIInc/crewAI/issues/6244

That referenced parent cause is missing or malformed. 4. CML reports broken causal lineage. This is different from observability/tracing: tracing can show what happened, while CML checks whether the action had a valid causal permission/responsibility path. raw_title: fix: log level warning → error for HTTP 4xx/5xx responses in automation service event forwarding
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from All-Hands-AI/OpenHands. Title: fix: log level warning → error for HTTP 4xx/5xx responses in automation service event forwarding Body snippet: ## Why `_send_source_to_automation_service` has three failure paths. PR #14820 raised the log level from `warning` to `error` for transport-level failures (`asyncio.TimeoutError`, `aiohttp.ClientError`), but the HTTP response failure path was left at `warning`: \`\`\`python # [REDACTED_LONG_TOKEN].py if resp.status >= 400: ... logger.warning( # <-- should be logger.error f'[AutomationEventService] Automation service returned ' f'{resp.status} for {source} org {org_id}: {body}' ) \`\`\` A `>= 400` response (including 408 Request Timeout, 504 Gateway Timeout, 5xx errors) means the event was not delivered — same user impact as a transport failure. Logging it as `warning` is inconsistent with the convention established in #14820: - `warning` = degraded but recoverable (retrying, falling back) - `error` = operation failed, user impact, no recovery ## What to change Change `logger.warning` to `logger.error` at the `resp.status >= 400` branch in `_send_source_to_automation_service`, and add a corresponding test to verify the level. ## Related Surfaced during review of #14820. Source URL: https://github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands/issues/14859

raw_title: API keys carry full user privileges — add scoped (reduced-privilege) credentials
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from All-Hands-AI/OpenHands. Title: API keys carry full user privileges — add scoped (reduced-privilege) credentials Body snippet: # API keys carry full user privileges — add scoped credentials ## Problem **Every API key inherits the complete permission set of the user who created it. There is no mechanism to issue a key with *fewer* rights than its owner.** This makes **all delegation all-or-nothing**: handing an API key to a script, an integration, a CI job, or the automation service hands over the user's *entire* account authority. ### The inverted-least-privilege trap The danger scales with the owner's privilege: > As an **org owner**, when I connect an automation I mint an API key. That key > carries my full org-owner rights, so the automation can do anything I can — > delete other members' automations, read all org data, manage membership — > even if it only needs to write to its own KV namespace. The people most > trusted to set up automations are exactly the ones whose delegated > credentials are most dangerous. A leaked or misbehaving key therefore has a blast radius equal to the *whole user account*, not the task it was issued for. ### Session keys are the same bug, automatically Sandbox **session API keys** are just keys minted on the user's behalf — they inherit full user permissions too (confirmed Source URL: https://github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands/issues/14912

Since FastAPI binds path parameters by name, `{id}` is never bound and `sandbox_id` is instead treated as a **required query parameter**. We both appreciate your opinion 😅 --- ## Summary As a follow-up to reviewing the recent Dependabot GitHub Actions bumps (#13700, #13959, #13960, #13961), I think we should harden our workflows against action-level supply-chain risk by pinning external third-party actions to immutable commit SHAs instead of mutable tags/branches, and separately review first-party mutable cross-repo refs like `OpenHands/extensions/plugins/pr-review@main`. This would reduce the blast radius of a compromised upstream release or a moved tag/branch, especially in workflows that have write permissions or access to secrets. ## Why now During the pass over workflow dependencies on `main`, I found several third-party actions still referenced by mutable versions (for example `@v7`, `@v4`) and one first-party action referenced by a mutable branch (`@main`). Highest-risk examples: 1. **First-party mutable cross-repo ref: `OpenHands/extensions/plugins/pr-review@main`** in `.github/workflows/pr-review-by-openhands.yml` - same-org / first-party, so this is not a third-party dependency in the same sense as the others below - Source URL: https://github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands/issues/14014

Title: [Bug]: V1 Web UI connects to agent-server via dynamic host port behind reverse proxy (Traefik), causing persistent "Network Error" / conversation stuck Body snippet: ### Is there an existing issue for the same bug? - [x] I have searched existing issues and this is not a duplicate. ### Bug Description Title: [Bug]: V1 Web UI connects to agent-server via dynamic host port behind reverse proxy (Traefik), causing persistent "Network Error" / conversation stuck Is there an existing issue for the same bug? I have searched the existing issues. Related/possibly duplicate: #12519, #12403, #12229. Describe the bug When deploying OpenHands V1 behind a reverse proxy (Traefik, via Dokploy) on a VPS, the application loads correctly, conversations are created, sandboxes spawn successfully, and skills load — but the conversation never becomes usable. The UI shows "Network Error" in the Changes panel and the message input stays "Disconnected" / "Waiting for sandbox". Backend logs are completely clean (all 200 OK, no errors after the auth fix below). The root cause is visible in the browser DevTools Network tab: the frontend issues requests directly to the agent-server using a dynamic host port instead of routing them through the reverse-proxied domain. Example failed request URL: https://openhands.example.com:42031/api/git/changes?path=%2Fworkspace%2Fproject Th Source URL: https://github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands/issues/14905

raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from browser-use/browser-use. Title: Bug: CDP connection instability causing indefinite hangs with remote browsers Body snippet: ### Browser Use Version 0.12.5 ### Bug Description, Steps to Reproduce, Screenshots # Individual CDP calls within handlers lack timeouts, causing indefinite hangs with remote browsers ## Summary When using browser-use with remote browsers via CDP (e.g., Browserless), individual CDP calls like `Runtime.evaluate` inside `DOMWatchdog.on_BrowserStateRequestEvent` can hang indefinitely if the CDP WebSocket connection becomes stale. There is no per-call timeout — the only safety net is the event-level timeout (default 30s), which wastes the entire timeout window before failing. ## Environment - browser-use 0.12.5 - Remote browser via CDP (Browserless) - `keep_alive=True` ## Observed behaviour When running sequential agents on the same browser session with `keep_alive=True`, there are naturally idle periods between agent runs where no CDP traffic flows. After such an idle period, the next `BrowserStateRequestEvent` can hang: 1. `DownloadsWatchdog` completes but its CDP call (`get_current_page_url`) takes **3.0s** instead of the usual **0.00-0.01s** — an early signal the connection is degraded 2. `DOMWatchdog` enters `_get_pending_network_requests` 3. `get_or_create_cdp_session(focus=True) Source URL: https://github.com/browser-use/browser-use/issues/4579

Title: bug: no automatic page-crash recovery — agent hangs after browser tab crashes Body snippet: ## Bug Description When a browser page crashes (e.g., due to memory exhaustion from heavy JavaScript, WebGL context loss, or renderer process crash), the agent has no automatic recovery mechanism. The agent hangs indefinitely waiting for page state that will never come. ## Steps to Reproduce 1. Configure browser-use agent to navigate a heavy SPA (e.g., complex React app with large state) 2. Allow the agent to interact with the page over multiple steps 3. When the page's renderer crashes (indicated by Playwright/Chromium page crash event), the agent: - Cannot detect that the page is dead - Continues trying to find elements that don't exist - Never reloads or restores state ## Expected Behavior The agent should: 1. Detect page crash events (via Playwright `page.on('crash')` or similar) 2. Automatically reload the page and attempt to restore navigation state 3. If recovery fails, report the crash as a tool error rather than hanging ## Actual Behavior Agent retries element selectors in a loop, eventually exhausting its step budget with "element not found" errors. The root cause (page crash) is never logged or surfaced. ## Relevant Code The page lifecycle is managed via Playwright, whic Source URL: https://github.com/browser-use/browser-use/issues/5067

raw_title: Agent governance: deterministic permission gating for browser actions
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from browser-use/browser-use. Title: Agent governance: deterministic permission gating for browser actions Body snippet: ## Context browser-use agents control a real browser — they navigate, click, fill forms, and extract data. Currently, any agent with browser access can perform any browser action. There's no mechanism to say "the product scraper agent may GET pages but not POST forms" or "the QA agent may click buttons but not navigate to non-whitelisted domains." The recent agchk audit (#4739) flagged this: `capability_policy` at HIGH and the observation that "Permission policy is not enforced on all dispatch paths." ## The problem Without deterministic permission gating, browser-use agents inherit the full browser surface. This creates risks: 1. **Domain escalation**: An agent tasked with scraping `example.com/docs` navigates to `example.com/admin` 2. **Action escalation**: An agent allowed to read page content instead submits forms or triggers downloads 3. **Data exfiltration**: An agent fills forms with sensitive data and submits to external endpoints 4. **Indirect prompt injection**: An agent navigates to a page with hidden instructions that redirect its behavior ## Proposal: Deterministic browser action gating Add an optional policy layer that evaluates before every browser action: [code bloc Source URL: https://github.com/browser-use/browser-use/issues/5063

raw_title: Feature Request: RFC: Standardized Evaluation/Benchmarking Harness for LLM Tool Calling
raw_prompt: Public GitHub issue from browser-use/browser-use. Title: Feature Request: RFC: Standardized Evaluation/Benchmarking Harness for LLM Tool Calling Body snippet: ### What is the problem that your feature request solves? Hi team - as Browser Use scales to support more models (Groq, local LLMs, GPT-4o), ensuring agent reliability across updates and different architectures becomes difficult without a baseline metric. I’d like to contribute a lightweight, standardized benchmarking suite in a new /evals directory to help the team catch silent regressions before they merge. Proposed Implementation: - A standalone script that runs the browser-use agent against 3-5 static, local HTML files (testing basic forms, deep DOM trees, and dynamic popups). - Automatically tracks and outputs a clean CLI table comparing: - Task success rate - Step count / Routing accuracy - Latency and token usage per run - Designed to be easily hooked into GitHub Actions/CI later so PRs can be tested against a known benchmark. Why me: I specialize in building custom evaluation harnesses for tool-calling agents. I recently built a similar suite for a LangGraph support system that successfully tracked routing accuracy and caught reproducible function-calling failures across different models (like malformed tool calls from Llama-3.3-70B). - Before I start writing the core logic Source URL: https://github.com/browser-use/browser-use/issues/5057

Title: BrowserSession fails on headless Linux: watchdog timeout and CDP WebSocket loop Body snippet: ## Environment - browser-use: 0.12.2 - Python: 3.12 - OS: Ubuntu 22.04, headless (no display, Xvfb available) - Chrome: running on port 9222 via CDP - Playwright: works fine standalone ## Problem Two separate failures on headless Linux servers: ### 1. Chromium launch: watchdog timeout (30s) [code block omitted] `LocalBrowserWatchdog.on_BrowserLaunchEvent` hangs and gets interrupted. Plain Playwright `launch(headless=True)` works on the same machine. Watchdog trace: [code block omitted] ### 2. CDP connection: WebSocket reconnection loop Connecting to existing Chrome via `BrowserSession(cdp_url="http://localhost:9222")`: [code block omitted] Plain Playwright `connect_over_cdp("http://localhost:9222")` works on the same Chrome instance. ## Impact browser-use CLI and MCP server (`--mcp`) are unusable on headless Linux servers, the primary deployment target for AI agent infrastructure. The `--connect` and `--cdp-url` flags in the Claude Code SKILL.md are not yet shipped in v0.12.2. ## Workaround Using Playwright directly (bypassing BrowserSession's watchdog/event layer) works for all operations. Source URL: https://github.com/browser-use/browser-use/issues/4471
